rulings.Case Title: IN RE: EZEQUIEL MONTANO LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Declaration Of Ezequiel Montano In Support Of The Motion To Quash Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, Aug-16-2024, Line 1, [PART ONE OF THREE] PETITIONER EZEQUIEL MONTANO LLC Declaration Of Ezequiel Montano In Support Of The Motion To Quash Subpoenas Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The City Attorney for the City and County of San Francisco has initiated an investigation into the marketing and sale of certain products touted to treat those conditions associated with diabetes. This investigation is evidently focused on whether persons and entities marketing certain diabetes-related products are violating California's Unfair Competition Law ("UCL"), Business and Professions Code sections 17200, et seq. As part of the City Attorney's investigation, he issued a subpoena compelling the production of a variety of business records by the Petitioner, an LLC which the record discloses is a business located in California's San Joaquin Valley. The City Attorney also served a second subpoena on Petitioner calling for it to respond to a set of business-related interrogatories. Petitioner initiated this action seeking an order quashing the two subpoenas. In support of its motion to quash, Petitioner first claims to have no business activities in or due process contacts with the City and County of San Francisco. Once this premise is laid, Petitioner contends that under controlling California law the City Attorney lacks the power or authority to pursue a UCL investigation outside the geographic limits of the city and county he serves. Petitioner concedes the state Attorney General certainly has the requisite authority to pursue a statewide UCL investigation, as evidently does a county District Attorney. See, Abbott Laboratories v. Superior Court, 9 Cal.5th 642 (2020). Petitioner argues, however, that a municipal city attorney does not have that same authority, citing People v. Hy-Lond Enterprises, Inc., 93 Cal.App.3d 734 (1979) and a cobbled-together combination of provisions of the California Constitution and caselaw interpreting the duties and responsibilities of a municipal city attorney. Petitioner also objects to the subpoenas as overbroad, vague and ambiguous, violative of third-party privacy interests and violative of Petitioner's rights under the Due Process Clause of the United States Constitution. [END OF PART ONE OF THREE] =(JPT/525)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA ACTING BY AND
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Administrative Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, Aug-16-2024, Line 2, [PART ONE OF TWO] PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA ACTING BY AND THROUGH SAN FRANCISCO CITY ATTORNEY DAVID CHIU Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Administrative Subpoenas. Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The City Attorney of San Francisco petitions for an order compelling Respondent to comply with administrative subpoenas in connection with an investigation being conducted by the City Attorney into suspected violations of the Unfair Competition Law, Business and Professions Code sections 17200, et seq. This action is the converse of Action No. CPF 24-518575 in which respondent Ezequiel Montano LLC is petitioning the Court to quash the same administrative subpoenas issued by the City Attorney. In Action No. CPF 24-518575, the Court has issued a tentative ruling denying the Petitioner's motion for an order quashing the two administrative subpoenas that are the subject of the instant action. Accordingly, for good cause shown, the Court hereby ORDERS respondent Ezequiel Montano LLC to comply with the two administrative subpoenas at issue herein. Respondent is ORDERED to produce the documents sought in the records subpoena issued by the City Attorney and to respond to the interrogatories posed in the second administrative subpoena. Compliance with both subpoenas shall be made no later than August 30, 2024. Although Petitioner also seeks an award of its costs incurred in the filing of its petition, the People (or the City) are exempt from paying filing fees, and the record is unclear as to what other costs are referred to in the Petition. For that reason, no costs are awarded. Counsel for Petitioner is directed to prepare a form of proposed order consistent with the foregoing and submit it to psw@hassard.com for review and execution. [END OF PART ONE OF TWO] =(525/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO JR VS. SHRYNE GROUP, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendant Shryne Group, Inc. To Srogs5 And Request For $2,000 Monetary Sanction; Declaration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Friday, August 16, 2024, Line 12. PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendant Shryne Group, Inc. To Srogs5 And Request For $2,000 Monetary Sanction. The judge pro tem's Report and Recommendation is adopted. In the event this matter is contested, counsel are ordered to appear in person in D.301 to argue the matter. Friday's Law & Motion Calendar will be called out of Dept. 301. Anyone intending to appear in person should report to Dept. 301. However, anyone intending to appear remotely should use the regular Zoom information for Dept. 302's Law & Motion Calendar for 9:30 a.m. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and attaches a copy of the report and recommendation and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RCE)

rulings.Case Title: NORMA CONTRERAS ET AL VS. CEMEX TRUCKING, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Plaintiff Medardo Ulloa And Document Production, And For Monetary Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, Aug-16-2024, Line 3, [PART ONE OF THREE] 1-DEFENDANT CEMEX CONSTRUCTION MATERIALS PACIFIC, LLC ERRONEOUSLY SUED HEREIN AS CEMEX TRUCKING, INC. AND CEMEX, INC. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Plaintiff Medardo Ulloa And Document Production, And For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This is a rather garden variety lawsuit arising out of a motor vehicle accident in which the plaintiffs claim injury caused by a collision with a cement truck on June 22, 2021 in San Francisco. The lawsuit was filed on May 18, 2022. Trial is set for November 4, 2024. Beginning in the Spring of 2024, counsel for both sides discussed dates for oral depositions of the three plaintiffs. Defendant noticed those depositions for two days in July, 2024. A day or two before the noticed deposition dates, however, plaintiffs' counsel contacted defense counsel and represented that he had no assurances that he was going to be able to produce his clients on the noticed dates. He offered explanations why his clients were experiencing logistical problems with attending pre-deposition meetings and getting to San Francisco for the in-person depositions. On the first noticed deposition day, defense counsel traveled to the deposition site in San Francisco from the East Bay to put plaintiffs' non-appearance "on the record." Prior to the first noticed deposition date, plaintiffs' counsel had proposed new dates in August. So, defense counsel renoticed the three depositions for August 21 and 22, 2024. In the meantime, defense counsel filed three motions: a motion for an order compelling the deposition appearance of each of the three plaintiffs, also seeking an order that the plaintiffs each produce a government-issued photo identification card to ensure the persons appearing were actually the plaintiffs. As a preliminary comment, the Court sees no reason for three separate motions being filed. One motion certainly would have sufficed, seeking issuance of a single order compelling the depositions of the three plaintiffs. That would have relieved the Court and plaintiff's counsel from having to read three virtually identical motions and plaintiffs' counsel from opposing each of the three motions. Defense counsel eventually seemed to understand the advantages of efficiency by filing a single reply memorandum. [END OF PART ONE OF THREE] =(525/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LUCIAN FILLER VS. CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Quash Or Otherwise Modify Medical Record Subpoenas And Quash The Deposition Subpoena Of Suzanne Hambleton
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, Aug-16-2024, Line 4, [PART ONE OF THREE] PLAINTIFF LUCIAN FILLER'S Motion To Quash Or Otherwise Modify Medical Record Subpoenas And Quash The Deposition Subpoena Of Suzanne Hambleton. Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff is seeking damages from the defendant California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC") premised on the several causes of action set forth in plaintiff's second amended complaint which include a claim plaintiff was terminated due to "whistleblower-related" activities by him relating to alleged fraud, abuse and waste at the CPUC uncovered by plaintiff. Plaintiff also alleges that CPUC officers and employees wrongly perceived him to be gay and in a homosexual relationship with another CPUC executive (also terminated) and that this perception was a material factor in his termination. Among other things, plaintiff seeks damages in compensation for emotional distress he claims to have suffered as a result of his treatment by the defendant. As part of its defense of plaintiff's claims, the CPUC subpoenaed plaintiff's medical records from several Dignity Health and UC Davis clinics covering a period from 2015 to the present. The CPUC also subpoenaed plaintiff's former wife, Ms. Hambleton, who divorced plaintiff in 2016, several years before plaintiff was hired by the CPUC. The CPUC justifies the medical records subpoenas and their evident breadth by contending plaintiff has rendered any and all of his treatment records since 2015 discoverable because he has put his CPUC-related emotional distress claims at issue in this litigation. The subpoena to plaintiff's former wife is explained by the CPUC as necessary to probe into a "rough" and "tough" divorce (potentially a stressor that would have some relation to plaintiff's damage claims in the lawsuit) and to delve into the details of plaintiff's relationship with the other terminated CPUC executive and/or her perceptions of plaintiff's sexual proclivities. The CPUC represents that it has been unable to subpoena the other executive and that plaintiff does not intend to call him as a witness at trial. Therefore, it needs to depose Ms. Hambleton in order to potentially discover relevant or admissible evidence. Before the Court is a motion by plaintiff to quash the medical records subpoenas and to quash the deposition subpoena to Ms. Hambleton. The CPUC opposes both motions to quash. [END OF PART ONE OF THREE] = (525/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES ZORDANI ET AL VS. GIGSTER INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Friday, August 16, 2024, Line 4. CROSS DEFENDANT ELASTECH SERVICES LLC's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS. Cross-defendant Elastech Services LLC's motion to quash service of summons is continued to January 16, 2025, to allow for jurisdictional discovery. (The Court's complete tentative ruling has been emailed to the parties.) Friday's Law & Motion Calendar will be called out of Dept. 301. Anyone intending to appear in person should report to Dept. 301. However, anyone intending to appear remotely should use the regular Zoom information for Dept. 302's Law & Motion Calendar for 9:30 a.m. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the Elastech is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RCE)

rulings.Case Title: ENERSPONSE, LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY VS. SMARTRENT.COM, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion In Support Of Its Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, Aug-16-2024, Line 6, [PART ONE OF TWO] DEFENDANT SMARTRENT TECHNOLOGIES,INC.'S, A DELAWARE CORPORATION Motion In Support Of Its Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff and defendant entered into a contract allowing defendant access to and use of plaintiff's DERMS platform and related data in order for defendant to provide enhanced services to its customers and, presumably, to be able to obtain new customers. The contract required defendant to pay an up-front payment (for licensing the plaintiff's cloud-based platform) and then monthly fees. For defendant, its principal consideration for entering into the contract was access to an additional revenue stream derived from so-called incentive revenues paid by utility companies when electricity use was reduced by parties like plaintiff and defendant during peak power periods. Defendant contends the parties agreed that it would receive 100% of these incentive revenues. But the contract did not contain language to that effect. As a consequence, once the contract was operating, plaintiff contended it was entitled to a significant part of those incentive revenues. When defendant stopped paying the monthly fees due under the contract, plaintiff initiated this action for breach of contract. Defendant cross-complained that it was entitled to the incentive revenues as allegedly agreed to during the pre-contract negotiations. Defendant then served discovery on plaintiff in the form of special interrogatories and a request for production of documents. When disputes arose as to plaintiff's compliance with the discovery, and after meet and confer efforts reached an impasse, defendant filed a motion to compel, seeking further responses to Special Interrogatories 21, 22, and 23 (identify other DERMS contracts for the years 2020 through 2022) and document production request numbers 49-51 (the other DERMS contracts) and 54-56 (revenues realized from those other DERMS contracts). First, plaintiff's initial objections to the at-issue discovery items are OVERRULED. Those objections, sounding in privacy, confidentiality and assertions that the information sought by defendant is not relevant to the subject matter of the action, are conclusory and untethered to the facts of this matter. A protective order entered herein satisfies any concerns re privacy and confidentiality. [END OF PART ONE OF TWO] =(525/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CLAUDIA ISABEL VARGAS CARDENAS VS. VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA, INC., A NEW JERSEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO REQUEST, DEMAND INSPECTION, PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, Aug-16-2024, Line 7, [PART ONE OF THREE] PLAINTIFF CLAUDIA VARGAS CARDENAS' , AN INDIVIDUAL MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO REQUEST, DEMAND INSPECTION, PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS. Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This action is a Lemon Law lawsuit by plaintiff against Volkswagen Group of America ("VW") with respect to asserted defects in the used 2021 VW ID.4 she purchased in 2023. There is no need for a summary of the plaintiff's allegations since counsel for both sides of this case are intimately familiar with the Song-Beverly Act provisions that give rise to plaintiff's complaint. Plaintiff propounded a First Set of Requests for Production of Documents to VW. VW responded, plaintiff exchanged meet and confer letters with VW's counsel focusing on alleged deficiencies in those responses. When meet and confer efforts met an impasse, plaintiff filed a motion to compel further responses to each of the 31 requests in the First Set. VW opposes the motion. Neither side requested an award of sanctions by the Court. [END OF PART ONE OF THREE] =(525/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JUAN CARLOS RODRIGUEZ VS. PER DIEM, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Initial Disclosures And Requests For Production, And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, Aug-16-2024, Line 8, [PART ONE OF THREE] PLAINTIFF JUAN RODRIGUEZ', AN INDIVIDUAL, ON BEHALF OF HIMSELF AND ALL SIMILARLY SITUATED EMPLOYEES Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Initial Disclosures And Requests For Production, And For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff has pleaded a putative class action against the defendant restaurants, and their managers/owners, alleging numerous Labor Code violations and claims of denial of minimum wage payments, tip "shaving" and improper pooling and the like. Plaintiff is a former server at one of the restaurants. Plaintiff served upon defendants initial demands for disclosure pursuant to a new discovery device embodied in Code of Civil Procedure section 2016.090 (unless indicated otherwise, all statutory references are to the Code of Civil Procedure). Plaintiff served initial disclosure demands on defendants Per Diem and PD Transbay under Section 2016.090(a)(1)(A), (B), (C) and (D). Plaintiff contends the defendants' responses were incomplete, evasive and not code-compliant, raising objections and conditional responses unauthorized by applicable law. Plaintiff asserts defendants' responses were also unverified (see Section 2016.090(a)(5), with the result that those responses should be treated as "no responses at all". Appleton v. Superior Court, 206 Cal.App.3d 632, 635-636 (1988). Addressing the issue of verifications first, the record does not show defendants' responses to plaintiff's initial disclosure demands were verified. For that reason, alone or in combination with the below reasoning, plaintiff's motion for an order compelling full, complete and verified responses to the initial disclosure demands is GRANTED. Moving on, defendants' several objections to the initial disclosure demands find no support on the record. The language of the initial demands made by plaintiff is set forth by statute. That language is not vague, ambiguous or otherwise objectionable. And, to emphasize another controlling point, the initial demand under Section 2060.090(a)(1)(A) does not seek the identification of "witnesses" but instead "persons likely to have discoverable information, along with the subjects of that information, that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, or that is relevant to the subject matter of the action or the order on any motion made in that action . . . [END OF PART ONE OF THREE] =(525/JPT)
